title: Verify dolt lock fixes (synthetic + real Ralph run)
description:
After all three implementation children land (flock wrapper, stale-LOCK cleanup,
bd_retry), verify the fix end-to-end. This task only exists to gate the epic on
real evidence the contention is gone.
design:
Three checks, in order:

1. Synthetic stress (proves flock wrapper works):
     for i in $(seq 50); do (bd ready >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo FAIL $i) & done; wait
   Expect: zero FAIL lines.

2. Real Ralph run + log inspection:
     ./ortus/ralph.sh --tasks 5
     grep -c "locked by another dolt" logs/ralph-*.log
   Expect: 0. (Per-call "Starting server with Config HP=..." lines remain —
   beads' ephemeral-server-per-call model is intentional. We only eliminate
   contention, not server churn.)

3. Stale-LOCK recovery:
     pkill -9 -f "dolt sql-server" || true
     touch .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK
     ./ortus/ralph.sh --tasks 1
   Expect: "Removed stale dolt LOCK: ..." log line, Ralph proceeds normally.

Record the resulting log filenames in the closing comment for this issue so
future regressions can be A/B-compared.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) Synthetic stress test (50 parallel bd calls) shows zero failures.
  (2) Fresh Ralph run log contains zero "locked by another dolt" lines.
  (3) Stale-LOCK recovery test: cleanup hook fires and Ralph proceeds.
  (4) Closing comment includes log filenames + grep counts for future
      regression reference.

Testing: see Design.

title: Clean stale dolt noms/LOCK files at Ralph startup
description:
When a `dolt sql-server` is killed mid-flight (sandbox teardown, OOM, ralph
timeout, ctrl-c), it can leave `noms/LOCK` files behind that prevent the next
Ralph startup from acquiring a fresh lock. We saw this in practice: 9+ stale
`dolt sql-server` processes accumulated in this project, one had been running
15+ hours, and bd became unable to connect at all until they were killed.

Add a startup hook to `ortus/ralph.sh` that removes stale LOCK files only when
no live dolt process is holding the parent directory open. Conservative by
design — we never yank the rug from a running server.
design:
Add this function near the top of `ortus/ralph.sh`, after `mkdir -p logs` and
before the sandbox smoke test:

  cleanup_stale_dolt_locks() {
    [ -d .beads/dolt ] || return 0
    while IFS= read -r -d '' lock; do
      # If any dolt sql-server PID has the LOCK's directory in its cmdline,
      # leave the file alone.
      if pgrep -af "dolt sql-server" 2>/dev/null | grep -qF "$(dirname "$lock")"; then
        continue
      fi
      rm -f "$lock"
      log "Removed stale dolt LOCK: $lock"
    done < <(find .beads/dolt -type f -name LOCK -print0 2>/dev/null)
  }
  cleanup_stale_dolt_locks

LOCK files observed in this codebase (cleanup must cover all three):
  - .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  - .beads/dolt/<project>/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  - .beads/dolt/<project>/.dolt/stats/.dolt/noms/LOCK

The `find` recursion above covers all of them automatically.

Side-recommendation (file separately if not covered here): also kill orphaned
`dolt sql-server` processes — but only ones with no parent process still
running. Less safe than LOCK cleanup; defer until we see it's needed.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `cleanup_stale_dolt_locks` is defined and called once at Ralph startup,
      before any iteration runs.
  (2) Each removed LOCK is logged to the ralph log file.
  (3) LOCK files are NOT removed when a live `dolt sql-server` process has the
      parent directory in its cmdline.
  (4) The function is a no-op when `.beads/dolt` doesn't exist (fresh project).

Testing:
  # Simulate a killed server's leftover LOCK:
  pkill -9 -f "dolt sql-server" || true
  touch .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  ./ortus/ralph.sh --tasks 1
  # Expect a "Removed stale dolt LOCK: ..." log line and Ralph proceeds.
close_reason: Added cleanup_stale_dolt_locks to ortus/ralph.sh; all 4 acceptance criteria verified (cleans canonical LOCK shapes, preserves live-server LOCKs, logs each removal, no-ops without .beads/dolt)

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- ortus/ralph.sh - Added `cleanup_stale_dolt_locks` function (defined + called) between the startup banner and the sandbox smoke test. Uses `find -print0` for safe iteration, `pgrep -af 'dolt sql-server' | grep -qF $(dirname $lock)` to skip locks held by live servers, and logs each removal via the existing `log` helper.

**Verification**: All 4 acceptance criteria validated.
- (1) Function defined + invoked once before iteration loop (ralph.sh:53-67).
- (2) Each removed LOCK logged: confirmed via fixture run (3 LOCK files in canonical paths .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/, .beads/dolt/<proj>/.dolt/noms/, .beads/dolt/<proj>/.dolt/stats/.dolt/noms/ — all removed, all logged).
- (3) Live-server preservation: spawned a fake `dolt sql-server --data-dir=…` process via `exec -a`, confirmed pgrep matched, ran cleanup, LOCK preserved.
- (4) No-op on missing .beads/dolt: ran in empty dir, no output / no errors.

Project gates: `bash -n` clean, `npm test` 1/1 pass, `npm run build` clean (tsc + vite). `npm run lint` not yet wired (eslint missing — tracked separately by bubbles-vyx.3).

**CodeGraph v1**:
modified: none (bash function in ortus/ralph.sh; CodeGraph does not index shell scripts in this project)
new: none
oos_callers: none

title: Add bd-locked flock wrapper + wire PATH in ralph.sh
description:
Add a small per-project flock shim that serializes all `bd` invocations against
a project-local lockfile. This is the highest-leverage fix from
`~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md` because it's the only one that defends against
concurrent bd calls from child Claude sessions inside Ralph (which we don't
control).

The shim is sandbox-friendly: no daemon, no network, no shared dolt server —
just a per-project file lock that turns "concurrent bd calls" into "tiny queue".
design:
New file: `ortus/bd-locked` (mode 0755):

  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  # Serialize bd invocations per project to avoid concurrent dolt sql-server
  # launches contending for .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK.
  set -euo pipefail
  root="$PWD"
  while [ "$root" != "/" ] && [ ! -d "$root/.beads" ]; do
    root="$(dirname "$root")"
  done
  lock="${root}/.beads/dolt.flock"
  mkdir -p "$(dirname "$lock")"
  exec flock -w "${BD_FLOCK_WAIT:-60}" "$lock" bd "$@"

Edit `ortus/ralph.sh`: after the XDG_CACHE_HOME exports (around line 138) and
before the main `while true` loop, prepend the project's ortus dir to PATH and
alias bd inside the loop's child env so child Claude sessions inherit the
serialized variant transparently:

  export PATH="$PWD/ortus:$PATH"
  # bd-locked shim handles serialization for all bd calls in this loop tree.

Note: do NOT rename the binary to `bd` — keep it as `bd-locked` and prepend the
ortus dir; we want users running `bd` outside Ralph to keep getting the unwrapped
binary unless they opt in. Ralph itself opts in by setting an alias inside the
child env, so child Claude sessions get the serialized version. (Discuss in
implementation: whether to alias or to symlink `ortus/bd -> bd-locked`. Either
works; pick one and document it.)
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `ortus/bd-locked` exists, is executable, and has the content above.
  (2) `ortus/ralph.sh` exports `PATH="$PWD/ortus:$PATH"` before the main loop.
  (3) Inside Ralph child sessions, `bd` resolves to (or aliases) the
      flock-wrapped variant.
  (4) Two simultaneous `bd ready` calls from a Ralph child session produce no
      "locked by another dolt process" errors — the second waits for the first.
  (5) `BD_FLOCK_WAIT` env var overrides the 60s default.

Testing:
  for i in $(seq 50); do (bd ready >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo FAIL $i) & done; wait
  # Expect zero FAIL lines; before this fix several would fail.
close_reason: Added ortus/bd-locked flock wrapper + bd symlink and wired PATH in ralph.sh; serializes bd invocations across Ralph child sessions to defeat dolt LOCK contention. Strict ACs (1)-(5) met; combined-system zero-FAIL behavior is m51.4's verification job once m51.3 retry helper lands.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- Added `ortus/bd-locked` (mode 0755): flock wrapper that serializes bd invocations against `.beads/dolt.flock`. Strips its own dir from PATH internally so `bd` resolves to the real binary, not the wrapper (avoids self-recursion when invoked via the `ortus/bd` symlink).
- Added `ortus/bd` symlink → `bd-locked`. Resolved the design's 'alias-vs-symlink' open question in favor of symlink: aliases don't propagate to subprocesses, and child Claude is a process (not a subshell), so PATH+symlink is the only path that actually serializes child-Claude bd calls.
- Edited `ortus/ralph.sh`: prepended `$PWD/ortus` to PATH after the cache-dir exports, before the main while loop.
- Added `dolt.flock` to `.beads/.gitignore` so the runtime lockfile isn't committed (`*.lock` doesn't match `*.flock`).

**Verification**: ACs (1)-(5) pass: bd-locked exists+executable; ralph.sh exports PATH=$PWD/ortus:$PATH at line 162 (before the while loop at 178); `PATH=$PWD/ortus:$PATH bd --version` resolves through ortus/bd → wrapper → real bd; BD_FLOCK_WAIT=5 honored via ${BD_FLOCK_WAIT:-60}; `bash -n` clean on both files; `npm test` 1/1 pass, `npm run build` clean. Caveat on the AC's testing block ("Expect zero FAIL lines" for 50-parallel): bd's per-call ephemeral dolt-server has an async LOCK release on shutdown, so even with flock-serialized bd invocations there's a residual race where dolt-server-N+1 sees dolt-server-N's stale LOCK file before it's flushed. The flock wrapper alone reduces but doesn't eliminate this — the m51.3 retry helper closes the gap by re-attempting the bd call once the previous LOCK clears. Verification of the combined system (m51.1+m51.2+m51.3) is m51.4's job. The strict 'no "locked by another dolt process" *errors surfaced to bd stderr*' contract from AC (4) is met: stderr capture across 50 parallel runs shows zero such matches.

**CodeGraph v1**:
modified: none (shell-only change)
new: none
oos_callers: none

title: Epic: Fix dolt lock contention in Ralph runs
description:
Ralph runs hit `database "dolt" is locked by another dolt process` errors because
beads v1.0.3 spawns a fresh `dolt sql-server` per `bd` invocation, and concurrent
bd calls race for `.beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK`. In one observed session
(logs/ralph-20260506-172900.log), 6 of ~56 bd calls failed (~11%), each retrying
~7 times. Stale LOCK files from killed servers compound the problem on restart.

Source analysis and recommended fixes: `~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md`. This epic
implements those fixes inside this project; the same fixes will be backported to
the ortus template separately so future generated projects don't have the issue.

Hard constraint: NO shared/long-lived `dolt sql-server` — neither external nor
in-sandbox. Every fix must work with beads' ephemeral-server-per-bd-call model
and only change how those calls are coordinated.
design:
Three coordinated fixes (children of this epic), in priority order:

  1. Per-project flock wrapper around `bd` (`ortus/bd-locked` + PATH wiring in
     ralph.sh). Highest leverage — defends against concurrent bd calls from any
     source including child Claude sessions.
  2. Stale `noms/LOCK` cleanup at Ralph startup. Removes LOCK files only when
     no `dolt sql-server` process is holding the parent directory.
  3. `bd_retry` helper around bd calls in shipped scripts; retries only on the
     specific lock-contention error strings, fails fast on anything else.

Plus housekeeping: track beads upstream for releases that reduce contention.

Verification (separate child issue) covers synthetic stress, real Ralph run
log inspection, and stale-lock recovery.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) All three implementation children (flock wrapper, stale-lock cleanup,
      bd_retry) are closed.
  (2) Verification child passes — fresh Ralph run produces zero
      "locked by another dolt process" lines in its log.
  (3) ortus parity: changes to `ortus/ralph.sh` are documented for backport;
      this epic links the corresponding work in `~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md`.

Testing: see verification child.

title: Privacy verification (no network calls with file contents)
description: Verify and codify PRD §8.4: no network requests carry user file content.
design: ESLint custom rule via no-restricted-syntax targeting CallExpression with callee fetch / XMLHttpRequest, scoped to parsing/ and pnl/. Document verification in README.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Manual: open Chrome devtools Network tab; drop the canonical CSV; confirm zero requests fire that contain file content. (2) Automated: an ESLint rule (or grep-based CI check) prevents fetch/XHR/WebSocket usage inside src/parsing/ and src/pnl/ modules. (3) README documents the verification procedure. Testing: run npm run lint after adding a fake fetch() call to parseCsv.ts → MUST fail; remove the call → passes; manually check Network tab during upload of a real file.

title: Wire pipeline: dropzone → parser → engine → chart
description: Connect the pieces in App.tsx: file from dropzone → parseCsv → computePnl → render results state with StatsStrip and BubbleChart.
design: App.tsx uses useReducer with state = {phase: 'empty'|'parsing'|'results'|'error', data?: ..., error?: string}. Async parse + compute happens in a single async function dispatched from onFile.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Dropping the canonical CSV transitions empty → processing → results without manual steps. (2) StatsStrip displays the PRD §13 numbers. (3) BubbleChart renders with 102 bubbles in contract view and 64 in ticker view. (4) ViewToggle works end-to-end. Testing: open npm run dev; drop the canonical CSV; verify stats strip matches §13; toggle views; verify bubble counts.

title: Support Contract & Ticker grouping views
description: <BubbleChart data={ClosedContract[] | ClosedTicker[]} groupingMode={'contract' | 'ticker'} /> picks the right tooltip field set and re-renders per ViewToggle.
design: Generic over the union; use a discriminator on groupingMode to select the field accessor functions.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) When groupingMode='contract', tooltip shows description + tradeCount. (2) When groupingMode='ticker', tooltip shows instrument + contracts (count). (3) Switching modes re-runs scales over the new dataset. (4) Bubble counts in axes and rendering correctly reflect the active dataset. Testing: in npm run dev, click ViewToggle to flip between modes; bubble count changes; tooltip fields change accordingly.

title: HoverTooltip component with edge-flipping
description: Implement <HoverTooltip /> per PRD §7.2 spec: white card, top border colored by gain/loss, fields: contract/ticker name, close date, P/L (signed), % return (signed), cost basis, closed qty, plus contract count (ticker view) or trade fill count (contract view). Flip to left side near right viewport edge per §7.3.
design: Position via absolute positioning over the SVG container; calculate x/y from the bubble's screen coords. Edge detection compares bubble x against container width minus tooltip width.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) On bubble hover, tooltip appears positioned just above the bubble. (2) Top border is gain-color or loss-color matching the bubble's sign. (3) All fields from §7.2 spec render. (4) When bubble is within ~150px of right viewport edge, tooltip flips to the bubble's left side. Testing: render chart; hover bubble in middle → tooltip on top; hover bubble near right edge → tooltip flips left; visually verify color matches sign.

title: Bubble rendering (color, size, render order)
description: Render bubbles per PRD §7.2: stroke #2E7D32 fill rgba(76,175,80,0.22) for gains; stroke #C62828 fill rgba(229,57,53,0.22) for losses; stroke 1.5px default, 2.5px on hover; rendered largest-first so smaller bubbles stay clickable.
design: <g> wrapping all <circle>s; bubbles passed as a sorted array. Hover state via React useState on hovered bubble id.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Each ClosedContract or ClosedTicker becomes one <circle> with cx,cy,r from scales. (2) Gain bubbles use the gain palette; losses use the loss palette. (3) Bubbles sorted by |pl| descending before render. (4) Hovered bubble's stroke widens to 2.5px. Testing: render fixture with 5 contracts (3 gains, 2 losses, varied |pl|); assert circle order in DOM matches |pl| desc; assert fill/stroke colors match spec; hover (via fireEvent.mouseEnter) → stroke-width changes to '2.5'.

title: D3 scales (time X, linear Y, sqrt radius)
description: Build the three scales used by the chart per PRD §7.2: time scale on close date (domain ±2 days), linear on % return (rounded to 250%), sqrt radius on |pl| (range [4, 42]).
design: Pure functions in src/components/chart/scales.ts so they're independently unit-testable. Use d3.extent() and Math.ceil/floor to round to 250 increments.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) buildXScale(data, width) returns a d3.scaleTime with domain expanded ±2 days. (2) buildYScale(data, height) rounds domain min/max to nearest multiple of 250 outside the extent. (3) buildRScale(data) is d3.scaleSqrt with domain [0, max(|pl|)] and range [4, 42]. Testing: pass fixture data with closeDate range Apr 2-30 and pctReturn -78.9 to +950.5; assert xScale.domain() === [Mar 31, May 2] and yScale.domain() === [-250, 1000].

title: SVG axes rendering (X weekly ticks, Y 250% increments, zero line)
description: Render the X and Y axes per PRD §7.2: X has weekly tick labels formatted 'MMM D' (e.g. 'Apr 9'), no axis line, no vertical gridlines; Y has horizontal gridlines every 250%, solid darker line (#3D4651) at y=0.
design: Hand-rolled SVG (per §8.1 'hand-rolled SVG for render'); use the scales from E5T1 to position lines and text. date-fns format() for X labels.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) X tick labels appear at one-week intervals across the data range, formatted via date-fns 'MMM d'. (2) Y gridlines drawn at every 250% boundary. (3) Y=0 gridline uses stroke #3D4651 distinguishably darker than other gridlines. (4) Tick labels formatted as 'N%'. Testing: render chart with fixture; inspect SVG output via React Testing Library's container.querySelectorAll('.tick'); assert correct count and formatting.

title: FileDropzone component (drag/drop, click-to-browse, .csv only)
description: Implement <FileDropzone onFile={(file) => void} /> per PRD §5.2 and §7.3 drag-over state.
design: Use a hidden <input type='file' accept='.csv'> bound to the dropzone click. Drag handlers (dragenter, dragover, dragleave, drop) maintain a local isDragOver state.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Clicking the dropzone opens the OS file picker filtered to .csv. (2) Dragging any file over the dropzone thickens the border and tints background faint blue. (3) Dropping a .csv calls onFile(file). (4) Dropping a non-CSV is rejected via the §9 'Only .csv files are supported.' banner. Testing: render in npm run dev; click → picker opens; drag a .csv from desktop → onFile fires; drag a .png → error banner shows.

title: App shell layout (1280px max-width, light gray background)
description: Build the page chrome from PRD §7.1: max-width 1280px centered container, light gray (#F5F6F8) page background, system font stack.
design: Top-level layout in App.tsx. Globals in src/styles.css. No CSS framework needed — page is a single column.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) <html> body background is #F5F6F8. (2) Main container max-width: 1280px, margin: 0 auto. (3) Font stack matches §7.2 system sans-serif. (4) On a 1920px viewport, content is horizontally centered with neutral gutters. Testing: npm run dev; inspect computed styles in devtools at 1280/1920/768 widths.

title: Regression test against canonical Robinhood validation dataset
description: Add the validation CSV from PRD §13 as a fixture and assert every metric in the appendix table reproduces within ±$0.01 / ±0.1%.
design: Store the CSV at src/pnl/__fixtures__/canonical-april-2026.csv. Test reads via fs.readFileSync at test time, parses via parseCsv, then computes — confirms the parsing+engine integration as well.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Closed contracts === 102; unique tickers === 64; total realized P/L === 368113.15 ±0.01; glRatio === 9.94 ±0.01; winRate === 83.3 ±0.1; avgWin === 4815.39 ±0.01; avgLoss === -2423.22 ±0.01. (2) Best contract identified as 'INTC 4/24/2026 Call $75.00' with pl 122565.66. (3) Worst contract identified as 'LITE 5/1/2026 Call $900.00' with pl -10643.14. Testing: npm test src/pnl/regression.test.ts — every assertion passes; deviation is treated as a regression failure.

title: Compute Summary stats (P/L, ratios, win rate, averages)
description: Produce the Summary object per PRD §6.3 from ClosedContract[]: totalPl, totalGain, totalLoss, glRatio, winRate, avgWin, avgLoss, avgPctWin, avgPctLoss, uniqueTickers, parseWarnings.
design: Single reduce over ClosedContract[]; partition winners (pl>0) and losers (pl<0); zero-pl ties counted as losers per PRD silent default. Document the tie behavior in a unit test.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) glRatio === totalGain / totalLoss when losses>0; null when totalLoss===0. (2) winRate === winnersCount / totalClosed * 100. (3) avgWin computed only over winners; avgLoss only over losers (signed negative). (4) avgPctWin/avgPctLoss are simple means of pctReturn over the respective groups. (5) parseWarnings echoes the parser's warnings array. Testing: fixture with 4 winners (sum +200) and 2 losers (sum -50) → summary.glRatio === 4, winRate === 66.67, avgWin === 50, avgLoss === -25.

title: Implement matched-closes per-contract algorithm
description: Implement the §6.2 algorithm in src/pnl/computePnl.ts producing ClosedContract[]. Group by (instrument, description); compute closed_qty/cost_used/proceeds_used/pl/cost_basis/pct_return/open_date/close_date.
design: Single linear pass: bucket by composite key, then compute per-bucket. Use a Map<string, RawTrade[]> keyed by .
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Given fixture trades with bto_qty=10, stc_qty=10, bto_amt=-1000, stc_amt=1500 → pl === 500, costBasis === 1000, pctReturn === 50, closedQty === 10. (2) Partial close (bto_qty=10, stc_qty=5) → closed_qty=5; cost_used proportional. (3) Open-only (stc_qty=0) → contract NOT in output. (4) BTC/STO/OEXP/CDIV trans codes IGNORED. Testing: write fixture-driven tests covering the four cases above; numeric equality via toBeCloseTo with 1e-9 tolerance.

title: Implement ticker-level aggregation
description: Aggregate ClosedContract[] by instrument into ClosedTicker[] per PRD §6.2 last paragraph: sum pl, cost_basis, closed_qty; count contracts; compute pct_return; min/max open/close dates.
design: Group ClosedContract[] by instrument via reduce into Map<string, ClosedContract[]>, then map to ClosedTicker via aggregate functions.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Two contracts under 'INTC' with pl=100 and pl=-50 → ticker.pl === 50; ticker.contracts === 2. (2) ticker.pctReturn === sum(pl) / sum(costBasis) * 100. (3) ticker.closeDate === most-recent contract closeDate; ticker.openDate === earliest contract openDate. Testing: fixture with 3 INTC contracts and 1 LITE contract → result has 2 tickers with correct sums and dates.

title: Define TypeScript types (RawTrade, ClosedContract, ClosedTicker, Summary)
description: Create src/pnl/types.ts with the four type definitions verbatim from PRD §6.3.
design: Plain interface/type aliases. Keep this file dependency-free so both parsing and pnl modules can import it without coupling.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) types.ts exports RawTrade, ClosedContract, ClosedTicker, Summary with field names and types matching §6.3 byte-for-byte. (2) transCode union includes the literals 'BTO' | 'STC' | 'BTC' | 'STO' | 'OEXP' | 'CDIV' | string. (3) Summary.glRatio is number | null. Testing: tsc --noEmit succeeds; create a sample object literal in a .test.ts file matching each shape — must compile without errors.

title: Implement parseCsv.ts wrapping PapaParse + RawTrade output
description: Wrap PapaParse to stream-parse a File or string into RawTrade[] using the normalizers from E2T1. Returns {trades, warnings}.
design: Stream via PapaParse step callback to avoid materializing huge files in memory; on completion, resolve with arrays. Catch row-level errors inside step and append to warnings without aborting.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) parseCsv accepts a File and returns Promise<{trades: RawTrade[], warnings: string[]}>. (2) Each emitted RawTrade has activityDate (Date), instrument, description, transCode, quantity (number), amount (signed number). (3) PapaParse is configured with header: true, skipEmptyLines: true, dynamicTyping: false. Testing: write a fixture CSV with 5 rows of varied trans codes; assert trades.length === 5 and field types match.

title: Header normalization & required column validation
description: Normalize CSV headers to canonical case-insensitive keys; reject the file (typed error) if any required column from PRD §6.1 is missing.
design: Lowercase + trim header strings, look up against a Map of canonical names to RawTrade keys. Check required set against present set; throw on first missing column.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Headers like 'activity date', 'Activity Date', 'ACTIVITY DATE' all map to the same key. (2) Missing 'Trans Code' throws ParseError with message exactly: 'Couldn\'t find required column: Trans Code. Is this a Robinhood activity export?' (per PRD §9). (3) Optional missing columns (Process Date, Settle Date, Price) do NOT throw. Testing: feed a CSV missing each required column individually; each MUST throw with the correct column name in the message.

title: Implement normalizers (parseAmount, parseQty, parseDate)
description: Implement src/parsing/normalizers.ts with three pure functions covering the edge cases in PRD §6.1: currency strings with $/commas/parentheses; quantity with trailing 'S'/commas; MM/DD/YYYY dates.
design: Pure synchronous functions; throw a typed ParseError for unrecoverable inputs (used by row-level malformed-row detection). Avoid date-fns parse for the hot path — handcrafted MM/DD/YYYY split is faster and unambiguous.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) parseAmount('($1,234.56)') === -1234.56; parseAmount('$50.00') === 50.00; parseAmount('') throws or returns NaN consistently. (2) parseQty('1,000S') === 1000; parseQty('5') === 5. (3) parseDate('04/24/2026') returns a Date matching 2026-04-24 UTC midnight; parseDate('not-a-date') throws ParseError. Testing: npm test src/parsing/normalizers.test.ts — all listed inputs pass; coverage of normalizers.ts ≥95%.

title: Initialize Vite + React 18 + TypeScript project (strict mode)
description: Scaffold the project with npm create vite@latest using the react-ts template. Enable strict TypeScript per PRD §8.1.
design: Use Vite's react-ts template as the baseline; tighten tsconfig immediately to avoid retro-fitting strictness later.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) package.json exists with react@18, vite, typescript dependencies. (2) tsconfig.json has strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes set to true. (3) npm run dev serves the default Vite React page on http://localhost:5173. Testing: run npm install && npm run dev — page loads in browser without console errors.
close_reason: Scaffolded Vite + React 18 + TypeScript with strict TS flags (strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes) per PRD §8.1; npm run dev serves on :5173; npm run build succeeds.

All 3 acceptance criteria met.

title: Epic: Integration, Privacy & Deployment
description: Wire the full pipeline (file → parser → engine → chart), validate the privacy promise (no network), prove performance targets (PRD §8.3), build a static bundle, and ship the methodology copy.
design: Use Performance.now() for in-app timing measurements logged to console behind a debug flag. Privacy verification is manual (devtools) plus a CI check that no fetch/XHR is called from parsing/pnl modules (eslint custom rule or grep).
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) End-to-end flow works on the validation CSV from PRD §13. (2) Network tab shows zero requests carrying file content. (3) 1k rows render in <500ms; 10k rows in <5s on a mid-range laptop. (4) npm run build emits a single static bundle deployable via npx serve dist. Testing: run the perf scripts in scripts/perf/, open browser devtools Network tab during upload to confirm no XHR/fetch fires with file bytes, and verify dist/ deploys to a static host preview.

title: Epic: P/L Computation Engine
description: Implement the matched-closes algorithm and ticker aggregation per PRD §6.2-6.3 in src/pnl/. Pure functions taking RawTrade[] and returning {contracts: ClosedContract[], tickers: ClosedTicker[], summary: Summary}. MVP excludes BTC/STO/OEXP/CDIV.
design: Group by (instrument, description); apply matched-closes math; aggregate by instrument; compute summary in a second pass. All math in number; document floating-point tolerance assumptions in computePnl.test.ts.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) computePnl(trades) returns the three arrays/object with shapes from §6.3. (2) Engine reproduces every number in PRD §13 (Appendix) within ±/tmp/claude/seed_bd.sh.01 / ±0.1%. (3) Open-only or sold-only positions are skipped silently. Testing: run npm test src/pnl — coverage ≥90%; the regression test against canonical Robinhood CSV MUST pass without snapshot diffs.

title: Epic: CSV Parsing Module
description: Build the pure-function parsing module (src/parsing/) per PRD §6.1 and §8.2. Owns header normalization, type-safe row coercion, malformed-row tolerance, and file-level guards. Outputs RawTrade[] + warnings; emits structured errors for unrecoverable problems.
design: PapaParse for streaming CSV; thin functional wrapper that maps raw records to RawTrade after normalization. Errors are typed (ParseError union); warnings are plain strings appended to a single array.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) parseCsv(file) returns {trades: RawTrade[], warnings: string[]} for a valid Robinhood export. (2) Required-column omission throws a typed error naming the missing column. (3) Malformed-row tolerance: parsing the validation CSV (1,029 rows, 1 malformed) yields 1,028 trades + a 1-row warning. Testing: run npm test src/parsing — coverage report MUST show ≥80% line coverage for parsing/.

title: Epic: UI Components & App Layout
description: Build the React component library and three-state page (empty/processing/results) per PRD §5 and §7. Includes FileDropzone, StatsStrip, ViewToggle, ErrorBanner, and the page shell. Excludes the chart itself (Epic E5).
design: Plain CSS or Tailwind (TBD per §8.1). Page state held in App.tsx via useReducer (state: empty|parsing|results|error). Components are presentational; App owns transitions.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) The three states render distinctly and only one is visible at a time. (2) Drag-over visually highlights the dropzone. (3) StatsStrip renders 5 tiles with the §7.4 contents from a Summary object. (4) ErrorBanner displays §9 messages verbatim. Testing: run npm run dev; manually walk Empty → Processing → Results → Error states using a sample CSV and a malformed CSV. Each transition MUST match §5.1.

title: Epic: Project Setup & Tooling Foundation
description: Bootstrap the Vite + React 18 + TypeScript project with strict mode, install runtime/test dependencies, configure linters, and scaffold the src/ module boundaries described in PRD §8.1-8.2. This epic is the prerequisite for all other implementation work; nothing else can start until the dev loop runs end-to-end.
design: Vite scaffold (npm create vite@latest -- --template react-ts), strict TS in tsconfig.json (strict, noUncheckedIndexedAccess, exactOptionalPropertyTypes), ESLint Airbnb + @typescript-eslint, Prettier, Vitest with jsdom env.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) npm run dev starts the Vite dev server and serves an empty React shell. (2) npm run lint passes with zero errors. (3) npm test runs Vitest with at least one passing smoke test. (4) src/{parsing,pnl,components} directories exist with index files. Testing: run npm install, npm run dev (visit http://localhost:5173), npm run lint, npm test — all four MUST exit 0 / show expected output.

title: Epic: Bubble Chart Visualization
description: Build the D3-scaled, hand-rolled SVG bubble chart matching the Schwab Realized Gain/Loss visual language per PRD §7.2. Includes axes, bubbles, hover tooltip, and Contract/Ticker grouping support.
design: d3-scale, d3-time, d3-array for math; React for SVG element rendering. Bubbles sorted by |pl| descending so smaller circles draw on top and remain hoverable. Tooltip rendered as an absolute-positioned <div> overlaying the SVG.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) X axis is time-scaled to data extent ±2 days with weekly 'MMM D' tick labels. (2) Y axis snaps to nearest 250%, has horizontal gridlines and a darker zero line (#3D4651). (3) Bubble area (sqrt-scaled radius 4-42px) is proportional to |pl|; gain/loss colors per spec. (4) Hovering a bubble shows the §7.2 tooltip with edge-flipping near right edge. (5) ViewToggle re-renders chart with the matching dataset. Testing: render a fixture with known data; inspect the SVG output via React Testing Library that tick labels, scales, and bubble counts match expectations. Visually verify at npm run dev.

title: Add bd_retry helper around bd calls in shipped scripts
description:
Defense-in-depth on top of the flock wrapper. Even with serialization, brief
ownership-handoff windows can race when one bd call is shutting down its
ephemeral sql-server while another is starting. Wrap bd invocations in shipped
scripts (`ortus/ralph.sh` and any others under `ortus/`) with a small retry
helper that retries ONLY on the specific dolt-lock error strings.

Critical: do not make this generic — retrying on every bd error would mask real
regressions.
design:
Add this helper to `ortus/ralph.sh`:

  bd_retry() {
    local n=0 max="${BD_RETRY_MAX:-5}" delay=0.25 out
    while :; do
      if out=$(bd "$@" 2>&1); then
        printf '%s\n' "$out"
        return 0
      fi
      if [[ "$out" == *"locked by another dolt process"* \
         || "$out" == *"database is locked"* ]] && (( n < max )); then
        sleep "$delay"
        delay=$(awk "BEGIN{print $delay*2}")
        n=$((n+1))
        continue
      fi
      printf '%s\n' "$out" >&2
      return 1
    done
  }

Replace direct `bd` calls in `ortus/*.sh` with `bd_retry` (e.g. any `bd dolt
pull` / `bd dolt push` invocations). Audit:
  rg 'bd ' ortus/

Document the helper near the top of ralph.sh so authors of new scripts pick it
up. Optional: extract to `ortus/bd_retry.sh` and source it from ralph.sh if the
list of consumers grows.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `bd_retry` is defined in `ortus/ralph.sh` (or sourced from a sibling).
  (2) Every bd invocation in shipped `ortus/*.sh` files uses `bd_retry`.
  (3) Retry triggers only on the two listed error substrings; other failures
      return immediately with stderr preserved.
  (4) `BD_RETRY_MAX` env var overrides the default 5 retries.

Testing:
  # Inject a fake lock error and verify retry path:
  cat > /tmp/bd-fake <<'BD'
  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  echo 'database "dolt" is locked by another dolt process' >&2
  exit 1
  BD
  chmod +x /tmp/bd-fake
  PATH=/tmp:$PATH bd_retry ready  # should retry 5x then fail with the lock msg
close_reason: Added ortus/bd_retry.sh and wired it into ortus/ralph.sh, idea.sh, and interview.sh. Lock-error-only retry with exponential backoff and BD_RETRY_MAX override; all 4 ACs verified (lock-retry timing, fast-fail on other errors, BD_RETRY_MAX, helper definition + universal usage). Defense-in-depth on top of m51.1 flock wrapper; unblocks m51.4 verification.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- New ortus/bd_retry.sh — defines bd_retry function (lock-error-only retry with exponential backoff, BD_RETRY_MAX override)
- ortus/ralph.sh — sources bd_retry.sh after the bd serialization PATH wiring, with comment pointing future scripts at the helper
- ortus/idea.sh — sources bd_retry.sh; bd create → bd_retry create (lines 93, 95)
- ortus/interview.sh — sources bd_retry.sh; bd list → bd_retry list (line 81), bd show → bd_retry show (lines 141, 262)

**Verification**: All 4 ACs verified end-to-end.
- AC(1) bd_retry defined in ortus/bd_retry.sh, sourced from ortus/ralph.sh.
- AC(2) grep confirms remaining 'bd ' matches in ortus/*.sh are the helper itself, allowedTools strings, and help text — no direct invocations.
- AC(3) Fake bd printing 'some other unrelated bd error' fails fast in 1ms with stderr preserved (no retries).
- AC(4) BD_RETRY_MAX=2 with fake lock-error returned in 801ms (~750ms expected: 0.25+0.5).
- Issue's testing snippet: PATH=fake bd_retry ready → exit 1 in 7836ms with original lock msg on stderr; matches 5-retry exponential schedule (0.25+0.5+1+2+4≈7.75s).
- Second lock substring 'database is locked' also triggers retry (1820ms with BD_RETRY_MAX=3 ≈ 0.25+0.5+1).
- bash -n: all four scripts parse clean.
- npm test: 1/1 passed. npm run build: ok (142.69 kB bundle). npm run lint: eslint not installed yet (bubbles-vyx.3, unrelated).

title: Vite build configuration & static deployment manifest
description: Ensure npm run build emits a single static bundle deployable to any static host per PRD §8.1 and acceptance criterion §10.9.
design: Vite default build config is sufficient. Add a single 'deploy' section to README listing the three host options.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) npm run build emits dist/ with index.html and hashed JS/CSS bundles. (2) npx serve dist serves the app and full flow works. (3) No environment variables required at build time. (4) README documents deployment to Vercel/Netlify/S3+CloudFront. Testing: rm -rf dist && npm run build && npx serve dist → load in browser, drop sample CSV, verify full flow.

title: Performance benchmarks (1k <500ms, 10k <5s, 60fps hover)
description: Build a perf harness per PRD §8.3: synthetic CSV generators at 1k and 10k rows, measure end-to-end parse+compute+render latency, log results.
design: Use Performance.now() around parseCsv and computePnl. Hover perf is observed manually via devtools — instructions in script comments.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) scripts/perf/gen-csv.ts produces deterministic 1k/10k row CSVs. (2) A perf test (script or Vitest bench) reports parse+compute latency for each. (3) On a mid-range laptop the 1k case is <500ms and 10k is <5s. (4) Manual test: hovering 100+ bubbles in dev maintains 60fps via Chrome perf panel. Testing: run npm run perf; verify printed latencies meet targets; record findings in perf/results.md (gitignored or just commit results).

title: Empty result state (no closed trades) within chart card
description: When ClosedContract[] is empty, replace the chart with the §7.3 friendly message: 'This file has no matched closes. All positions appear to still be open.'
design: Conditional render at the top of <BubbleChart> before scale construction. Avoid rendering an empty SVG.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Chart card renders the message centered when data.length === 0. (2) Axes are NOT rendered in this state. (3) StatsStrip still renders with '—' placeholders. Testing: load a fixture CSV with only BTO trades (no STC) → chart card shows the message; stats tiles show dashes.

title: Results state container with 'Upload another' affordance
description: Compose the results page per PRD §5.1: stats strip on top, chart card below, methodology footnote, 'Upload another' button to return to empty state.
design: Container component pulling state from App.tsx's reducer. The chart slot is filled by <BubbleChart /> from Epic E5.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Results state shows StatsStrip, then chart card (chart implementation lives in E5), then methodology footnote text. (2) 'Upload another' button visible; clicking returns app to empty state and clears in-memory data. Testing: walk through full flow with sample CSV; confirm layout order matches §5.1; click 'Upload another' → empty state returns; reload data — no leak from previous parse.

title: StatsStrip with 5 stat tiles
description: Implement <StatsStrip summary={Summary} /> rendering the five tiles per PRD §7.4 (Gain/Loss Ratio, Total Realized P/L, Win Rate, Avg Win/Loss, Avg % Return).
design: Pure presentational; all formatting via Intl.NumberFormat helpers in src/components/format.ts. Sub-text values use 'compact' notation ($4.8K).
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Five white cards in a horizontal flex row. (2) Each card has uppercase tiny label, 22px primary value, smaller sub-text. (3) Values formatted exactly per §7.4: gain/loss ratio to 2 decimals, P/L as ±$N,NNN.NN, win rate as N.N%, etc. (4) When summary has zero closed positions, tiles show '—' placeholders per §9. Testing: render with the canonical-summary fixture; visually confirm against the §7.4 table; render with empty summary; verify dashes.

title: ViewToggle (Contract/Ticker segmented control)
description: Implement <ViewToggle value={'contract'|'ticker'} onChange counts={{contract, ticker}} /> per PRD §7.1: top-right of chart card, two-button segment showing 'By Contract · N' and 'By Ticker · N'.
design: Standard role='radiogroup' segmented control. Styled with simple CSS — no design system.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Two buttons rendered, the active one visually pressed. (2) Each button label shows the count, e.g. 'By Contract · 102'. (3) Clicking a button calls onChange with the new value. (4) Default is 'contract'. Testing: render with counts {contract: 102, ticker: 64}; click 'By Ticker · 64' → onChange called with 'ticker'; active state visually flips.

title: ErrorBanner component
description: Reusable <ErrorBanner message={string} onDismiss={() => void} /> for the §9 error conditions.
design: Position fixed at top, full width within the 1280px container. Use role='alert' for accessibility.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Renders a top-of-page red banner with the provided message verbatim. (2) Has a dismiss (×) button calling onDismiss. (3) Does NOT auto-dismiss. Testing: render with each §9 message; click ×; confirm onDismiss fires; confirm banner unmounts on parent state change.

title: Empty state UI (drop zone copy, privacy note, sample link)
description: Render the empty-state copy verbatim from PRD §5.1 and §7.3, including the 'Use sample data' link and privacy note 'Your data never leaves this browser.'
design: Empty state is a child of <FileDropzone>; sample link triggers a fetch of /sample.csv asset (asset created in E6T4).
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Empty state shows 'Drag your Robinhood activity CSV here, or click to browse.' (2) Privacy line: 'Your data never leaves this browser.' (3) 'Use sample data' link is present and clickable. (4) On render, dropzone occupies the chart area (matching §7.3). Testing: load npm run dev with no file uploaded; assert all three strings appear in the DOM; visually confirm dropzone is the dominant element.

title: Processing state UI (spinner, progress bar)
description: Render parsing state per PRD §5.1 and §7.3: dropzone collapses; inline status 'Parsing N rows…'; if parse takes >2s, show a determinate progress bar.
design: PapaParse 'step' callback emits row counts that drive the progress bar via setState. Use Intl.NumberFormat for the comma separator.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Once a file is dropped, dropzone collapses and 'Parsing 1,029 rows…' (formatted with comma) appears. (2) After 2s of parsing, a progress bar (0-100%) is visible. (3) On parse completion, processing state hides. Testing: drop the canonical 1,029-row CSV; observe state transition. Force a 3s artificial delay in parser to verify progress bar appears.

title: Unit tests for engine (≥90% line coverage)
description: Comprehensive unit tests for computePnl.ts and aggregation/summary helpers, covering happy paths and edge cases.
design: Use shared fixture helpers: makeTrade(opts) and trades fixture builders. Co-locate tests with sources in src/pnl/.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Vitest --coverage shows pnl/ at ≥90% line coverage. (2) Tests include: full close, partial close, open-only skip, sold-only skip, multi-contract ticker aggregation, glRatio===null when no losses, single-contract ticker. Testing: npm test -- --coverage pnl — coverage gate ≥90%; all tests pass.

title: File-level validation (.csv extension, ≤50MB, empty file)
description: Pre-parse guards from PRD §9: reject non-CSV files, files >50MB, and empty files with the exact banner messages.
design: Guards live in parseCsv.ts at the top of the function. Use file.name.toLowerCase().endsWith('.csv') and file.size in bytes; constants for the 50MB threshold (50 * 1024 * 1024).
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Non-.csv file rejected with 'Only .csv files are supported.' (2) File of 0 bytes rejected with 'This file is empty.' (3) File >50MB rejected with 'File is unusually large for an activity export. Max 50MB.' (4) Validation runs BEFORE PapaParse is invoked. Testing: feed each rejection case; assert error.message matches verbatim. A 100-byte valid CSV passes through to parsing.

title: Unit tests for parser (≥80% line coverage)
description: Comprehensive unit tests over parsing/, including all PRD §6.1 edge cases, the malformed-row case, header variations, and file-level guards.
design: Use beforeEach to construct File objects from in-memory strings (new File([str], 'name.csv')). Snapshot warning arrays where useful.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) parseCsv.test.ts and normalizers.test.ts together cover ≥80% of parsing/ lines per Vitest --coverage. (2) Tests cover: currency parens, comma thousands, trailing 'S' on qty, all required-column-missing variants, malformed-row aggregation, file-too-large, non-CSV, empty file. Testing: run npm test -- --coverage parsing — coverage report MUST show parsing/ at ≥80%; all tests pass.

title: Skip malformed rows with warning aggregation
description: When a row fails normalization (e.g. extra field, bad date), skip it and increment a counter; at end, emit a single aggregated warning per PRD §6.1 edge case 3.
design: Increment a counter inside the step callback's try/catch; format the final warning string after parse completes. Use Intl.PluralRules or simple n===1 check for 'row' vs 'rows'.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Parsing the canonical CSV (1,029 rows, 1 malformed) yields 1,028 trades + warnings === ['1 row skipped: malformed']. (2) Multiple malformed rows aggregate: '3 rows skipped: malformed'. (3) Empty trailing rows are silently dropped (NOT counted in warnings). Testing: fixture CSVs with 0/1/3 malformed rows + 2 trailing empty rows MUST produce the expected warning strings exactly.

title: Set up Vitest + React Testing Library
description: Configure Vitest with jsdom env and React Testing Library per PRD §8.1 ('Testing: Vitest + React Testing Library').
design: Use Vitest in-source coverage via @vitest/coverage-v8. Include a smoke test from the start so future tasks have a working harness to copy from.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) vitest.config.ts (or vite.config.ts test block) sets environment: 'jsdom', globals: true. (2) @testing-library/react, @testing-library/jest-dom, jsdom are devDependencies. (3) src/setupTests.ts imports '@testing-library/jest-dom'. (4) An example src/App.test.tsx renders the app and asserts a known string. Testing: run npm test — at least one test passes; npm test -- --coverage emits a coverage/ folder.
close_reason: Vitest + RTL configured per PRD §8.1: jsdom env, globals, setupTests.ts, App.test.tsx smoke test passing; coverage harness produces coverage/ folder.

- Updated vite.config.ts to defineConfig from vitest/config with test block (environment: jsdom, globals: true, setupFiles)

- Created src/setupTests.ts importing '@testing-library/jest-dom'
- Created src/App.test.tsx rendering <App /> and asserting the 'PnL Bubbles' heading

**Verification**: npm test → 1/1 passing in 661ms; npm test -- --coverage → coverage/ folder generated (App.tsx 100%); npm run build → tsc -b clean, vite build clean (142.69 kB JS). All 4 acceptance criteria met.

title: Scaffold src/ module structure (parsing/, pnl/, components/)
description: Create the directory structure from PRD §8.2 with empty index files so import paths can be reserved before implementation begins.

acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) src/parsing/, src/pnl/, src/components/ exist. (2) Each contains an index.ts that exports nothing (empty namespace placeholder) without TS errors. (3) main.tsx mounts <App /> from App.tsx. Testing: run npm run build — clean exit, dist/ produced; run npx tsc --noEmit — zero errors.

title: Configure ESLint + Prettier (Airbnb style)
description: Wire up ESLint with eslint-config-airbnb-typescript and Prettier per PRD §8.1 'Linter: eslint' and the Code Standards (Airbnb).
design: Use eslint-config-airbnb-typescript@^18 paired with @typescript-eslint/parser. Prettier conflicts disabled via eslint-config-prettier (last in extends order).
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) .eslintrc.cjs extends airbnb, airbnb-typescript, and prettier. (2) .prettierrc.json exists. (3) npm run lint and npm run format scripts in package.json. (4) A deliberately bad file (let x = 1; var y = 2;) flags ESLint errors. Testing: run npm run lint on a clean tree → exit 0; introduce a 'var' declaration → lint MUST fail; remove it; lint passes again.

title: Install runtime dependencies (PapaParse, D3 modules, date-fns)
description: Add the runtime libraries called out in PRD §8.1: papaparse, d3-scale, d3-time, d3-array, date-fns, plus their @types/* packages.
design: Pin to current major versions (d3-scale@^4, papaparse@^5, date-fns@^3). Tree-shaking is essential — do NOT install the umbrella d3 package.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) package.json dependencies include papaparse, d3-scale, d3-time, d3-array, date-fns. (2) devDependencies include @types/papaparse, @types/d3-scale, @types/d3-time, @types/d3-array. (3) A trivial import { scaleLinear } from 'd3-scale' compiles without TS errors. Testing: write a one-line src/smoke.ts importing one symbol from each lib; npm run build succeeds.

title: silent-test
description: t

title: test
description: test

title: Track beads upstream for lock-contention fixes; bump pin when available
description:
beads v1.0.3 is the version on this machine and is the version that exhibits
the per-call ephemeral-sql-server churn. Future beads releases may reduce
contention upstream (e.g., better in-memory coordination, faster server
startup, sqlite-backed mode). Track https://github.com/steveyegge/beads
releases and bump the recommended/pinned version when something relevant lands.

Hard constraint reminder: even if upstream adds a daemon/keepalive mode, we do
NOT adopt it — no shared/long-lived dolt server, ever. Only adopt upstream
features that work with the ephemeral-server-per-call model.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) When a new beads minor/patch release lands, skim the changelog for
      lock-related fixes (lockfile handling, server startup, contention).
  (2) If a relevant fix exists, test against this project and bump the pinned
      version in any project bootstrap docs.
  (3) Close with a comment noting the version reviewed and the decision.

Recurring chore — close and re-open per release cycle, or convert to a recurring
patrol-type issue if beads add their own lock-contention milestone.

title: Methodology footnote text in results state
description: Add the §6.2 'matched-closes' methodology summary as a small footnote under the chart card, plus the parse-warnings chip from §9 'Some rows malformed'.
design: Footnote is plain HTML below the chart card. Warning chip uses a <details>/<summary> for the expand interaction.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) Results state has a footnote: 'P/L computed via matched closes: for each contract, min(BTO qty, STC qty) is closed at proportional cost basis. Open or sold-only positions are excluded.' (2) When summary.parseWarnings is non-empty, a small warning chip appears: 'N rows were skipped.' Clicking expands the warnings list. Testing: run with a CSV that has 1 malformed row → chip shows '1 row was skipped.'; click to expand → see full warning string.

title: Create sample CSV asset for 'Use sample data' link
description: Generate or curate a small (~50 row) Robinhood-format CSV that demonstrates the chart with both winners and losers, served from public/sample.csv.
design: Hand-craft to ensure visual interest: include one big winner, several modest gains, two clear losses, varied dates across 4 weeks.
acceptance_criteria: Conditions: (1) public/sample.csv exists with at least 30 BTO/STC pairs across 5+ tickers. (2) When loaded via the empty-state link, app reaches results state with a populated chart. (3) Sample contains no real PII — synthetic ticker symbols only. Testing: click 'Use sample data' in npm run dev → chart renders with bubbles; sanity-check stats tiles show realistic numbers.
